test_id_1.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 is older and mainly points providers back to the manual and Medicaid Update articles for pharmacy claims guidance. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says that for pharmacy claims, providers should refer to the eMedNY Pharmacy Billing Guidelines and the ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual: “For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy” and “Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]. For dual eligible members, the policy context also states that “Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare” and that “Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members” [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]. The February 2024 Medicaid Update adds that “Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence states that “the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient” and that if “the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also states that Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers are “required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members” and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from properly enrolled providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources treat dual eligible pharmacy-related billing as involving Medicare rather than Medicaid-only billing: the policy says “Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare,” and the manual says providers must “submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members” [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate that Medicaid payment is limited or secondary in this context: the policy says “Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members,” while the manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment “will not exceed” the Medicaid-only amount and “no additional payment will be made” if Medicare pays more [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy excerpts are narrower and older, focusing on immunization access and stating that pharmacies may bill only for “Medicaid non-dual eligible members,” while the provider manual is broader and newer, addressing Medicare enrollment, claim submission, assignment, and payment limits for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual gives the current operational rule for dual eligible pharmacy claims, including that pharmacies must “submit claims and accept assignments” for Medicare-covered services and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment; the policy excerpts do not provide that level of claim-processing detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023].
comparison: Both sources treat dual eligible pharmacy-related billing as involving Medicare rather than Medicaid-only billing: the policy says “Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare,” and the manual says providers must “submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members” [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate that Medicaid payment is limited or secondary in this context: the policy says “Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members,” while the manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment “will not exceed” the Medicaid-only amount and “no additional payment will be made” if Medicare pays more [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts are narrower and older, focusing on immunization access and stating that pharmacies may bill only for “Medicaid non-dual eligible members,” while the provider manual is broader and newer, addressing Medicare enrollment, claim submission, assignment, and payment limits for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual gives the current operational rule for dual eligible pharmacy claims, including that pharmacies must “submit claims and accept assignments” for Medicare-covered services and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment; the policy excerpts do not provide that level of claim-processing detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a single, current, comprehensive definition of dual eligible pharmacy claims; they mainly reference where to find billing guidance and older immunization-related instructions. The provider manual is newer and should guide current dual eligible pharmacy claim handling, but the policy excerpts remain relevant background for the limited points they expressly state. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_1.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_1.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_1.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is newer and should guide current practice for this issue unless the user is asking about historical policy language.

years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.
Through monitoring real-time claims, NYRx has identified two trends in pharmacy submitted 340B
drug claims:
1.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21-22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy source does not itself define dual eligible pharmacy claim billing rules in detail; it says, \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy\" and also references the ProDUR/ECCA provider manual for claims guidance. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] For dual eligible members, older Medicaid Update guidance states, \"Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare.\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] The newest policy source also states, \"Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines\" and Medicaid Update articles. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance states that \"the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and that \"If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] It also states, \"all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from properly enrolled providers. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources direct dual eligible pharmacy-related services away from Medicaid-only billing and toward Medicare-related handling: the policy says, \"Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare,\" and the manual requires providers to \"submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members.\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources indicate that Medicaid pharmacy billing is not standalone for dual eligible situations and that providers should follow the applicable pharmacy/manual guidance rather than relying only on the update article: the policy says, \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy,\" and the manual sets the payment and enrollment rules for dual eligible claims. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The policy excerpts are limited and do not provide a full dual eligible pharmacy-claim payment rule; they mainly point readers to other billing guidance and, in older language, say dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]", "The provider manual gives the operative current claim/payment rule for dual eligible pharmacy services: Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount, and if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, \"no additional payment will be made.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "caveats": "The policy excerpts provided are sparse and mostly cross-reference other manuals, so they do not fully define dual eligible pharmacy claim handling on their own. The provider manual is newer and should guide current practice for this issue unless the user is asking about historical policy language. "}
test_id_2.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_2.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_2.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_3.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 is older and mainly points providers back to the manual and Medicaid Update articles for pharmacy claims guidance. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence does not define dual eligible pharmacy claim billing in detail; it says, "Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines... and Medicaid Update articles" [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]. Older policy guidance states, "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states, "the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made"; it also says, "the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. It further states that "all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources treat dual eligible pharmacy-related billing as tied to Medicare rather than Medicaid-only billing: policy says "Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 9:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and the manual says providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate Medicaid payment is limited or secondary in this context: policy says pharmacies "will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], while the manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed" the Medicaid-only amount and that no additional payment is made if Medicare pays more [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy source is narrower and older, focusing on immunization access and stating that pharmacies "will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], while the provider manual is broader and newer, addressing Medicare/Medicaid payment limits and claim submission/assignment obligations for Medicare-covered services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy source does not provide the detailed payment rule found in the manual; the manual states "the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025], which is not stated in the policy excerpt provided.
comparison: Both sources treat dual eligible pharmacy-related billing as tied to Medicare rather than Medicaid-only billing: policy says "Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 9:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and the manual says providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate Medicaid payment is limited or secondary in this context: policy says pharmacies "will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], while the manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed" the Medicaid-only amount and that no additional payment is made if Medicare pays more [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy source is narrower and older, focusing on immunization access and stating that pharmacies "will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], while the provider manual is broader and newer, addressing Medicare/Medicaid payment limits and claim submission/assignment obligations for Medicare-covered services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy source does not provide the detailed payment rule found in the manual; the manual states "the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025], which is not stated in the policy excerpt provided.
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for the policy source beyond the limited immunization/billing statements quoted above. The newest provider manual source is more recent and should guide current handling of dual eligible pharmacy claims unless the user is asking about the older 2021 policy context.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.

"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."

For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_3.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_4.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 points providers to the pharmacy manual for pharmacy claims, while older Medicaid Update language says dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt does not define dual eligible pharmacy claims in detail; it says, "For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]. For dual eligible members in the older Medicaid Update guidance, "Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" and "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that "the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, "no additional payment will be made"; it also says "the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. It further states that "all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources recognize that dual eligible members involve Medicare in pharmacy-related billing/payment rules: the policy says "Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and the provider manual says providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate Medicaid payment is limited or secondary in this context: the policy says "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and the provider manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed" the Medicaid-only amount and "No additional payment will be made" if Medicare payment is greater [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy excerpts are narrower and older, focusing on immunization access and directing pharmacy claims readers elsewhere: "For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023], while the provider manual gives direct payment and claim-submission rules for dual eligible pharmacy services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18; 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more current and more specific for this issue: it requires pharmacies to "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the policy excerpt only says dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare and does not provide the same claim-processing detail [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021].
comparison: Both sources recognize that dual eligible members involve Medicare in pharmacy-related billing/payment rules: the policy says "Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and the provider manual says providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate Medicaid payment is limited or secondary in this context: the policy says "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and the provider manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed" the Medicaid-only amount and "No additional payment will be made" if Medicare payment is greater [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts are narrower and older, focusing on immunization access and directing pharmacy claims readers elsewhere: "For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023], while the provider manual gives direct payment and claim-submission rules for dual eligible pharmacy services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18; 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more current and more specific for this issue: it requires pharmacies to "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the policy excerpt only says dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare and does not provide the same claim-processing detail [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021].
answers.caveats: The policy excerpts provided do not contain a full current definition of dual eligible pharmacy claims; the April 2023 policy chunk explicitly points to the pharmacy billing guidelines instead [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]. The provider manual is the more recent and more detailed source for current guidance on this issue [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18; 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
