test_id_5.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 is older and points providers to the pharmacy manual and Medicaid Update articles for pharmacy claims guidance. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy evidence does not define dual eligible pharmacy claims in detail; it says, \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy\" and also to the ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual for claims guidance. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] The older policy update states, \"Dual-eligible enrollees will continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" and that \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members,\" which is limited to immunization services rather than general pharmacy claims. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance states that \"the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and that \"If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers \"are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from properly enrolled providers. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources tie dual eligible pharmacy-related billing to Medicare rather than treating Medicaid as the only payer: the policy says \"Dual-eligible enrollees will continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" and the manual says providers must \"submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members.\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources indicate Medicaid payment is limited or secondary for dual eligible situations: the policy says \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members,\" and the manual says \"The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient.\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The policy excerpts are narrower and older, focusing on immunization services and directing billing questions elsewhere: \"Dual-eligible enrollees will continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" and \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy.\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]", "The provider manual gives more current and operational dual eligible pharmacy claim rules, including Medicare enrollment and payment limits: \"the NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid\" and \"If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22, 18 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "caveats": "The provided policy excerpts do not contain a full current definition of dual eligible pharmacy claims; they mainly point readers to other billing manuals and include an older immunization-specific rule. The provider manual is the more recent and more directly applicable source for current dual eligible pharmacy claim guidance. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]"}
test_id_5.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_5.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_5.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_6.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid, but the provider manual gives the clearest current rule that pharmacies must submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid because "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and that "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance also states that "It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that "Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says that if claim issues are resolved, "the pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] The billing guidelines add that it is "the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18-19 — Jul 23, 2025]
similarities: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and "Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" and the pharmacy may resubmit to Medicaid only "after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy source states the general rule that providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, but it does not spell out the pharmacy-specific "per the plan’s requirements" language found in the provider manual. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual adds operational detail that the pharmacy must determine whether the patient has other insurance and whether that insurer covers the service being billed, which is not stated in the policy excerpt provided. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18-19 — Jul 23, 2025] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.
comparison: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and "Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" and the pharmacy may resubmit to Medicaid only "after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy source states the general rule that providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, but it does not spell out the pharmacy-specific "per the plan’s requirements" language found in the provider manual. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual adds operational detail that the pharmacy must determine whether the patient has other insurance and whether that insurer covers the service being billed, which is not stated in the policy excerpt provided. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18-19 — Jul 23, 2025] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not include a dual-eligible-specific pharmacy claim rule beyond the general payer-of-last-resort/TPL billing requirement, so the policy side is broader than the provider manual. The newest provider manual source is later than the newest policy source, so it should guide current operational billing practice unless the user is asking about a historical period. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_6.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
test_id_6.pm_ground_truth: For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
test_id_6.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

test_id_7.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_7.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid, but the provider manual gives the clearest current rule that pharmacies must submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid because "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and that "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance also states that "It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that "Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says that if billing a plan is difficult, the pharmacy should advise the prescriber and member of delays and may resubmit to Medicaid only after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources place primary billing responsibility on the pharmacy/provider to pursue other liable coverage before Medicaid: "bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and "submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other coverage: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and the provider manual requires other coverage to be billed first before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy source states the general payer-order rule in broad terms—"bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid"—while the provider manual is more specific to pharmacy claims and says the pharmacy must submit to "a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements" before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual adds operational detail not stated in the policy excerpt: if plan billing has problems, the pharmacy should "advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution" and may resubmit to Medicaid only after proper TPL adjudication. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpt does not provide that level of resubmission detail. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources place primary billing responsibility on the pharmacy/provider to pursue other liable coverage before Medicaid: "bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and "submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other coverage: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and the provider manual requires other coverage to be billed first before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy source states the general payer-order rule in broad terms—"bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid"—while the provider manual is more specific to pharmacy claims and says the pharmacy must submit to "a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements" before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual adds operational detail not stated in the policy excerpt: if plan billing has problems, the pharmacy should "advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution" and may resubmit to Medicaid only after proper TPL adjudication. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpt does not provide that level of resubmission detail. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not include a pharmacy-manual-style operational workflow for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, so the policy side is broader than the provider manual. The question asks about dual-eligible pharmacy claims specifically, but the clearest grounded dual-eligible-specific language in the provided chunks is the provider manual’s pharmacy/TPL billing rule; the policy excerpt supports the general payer-last-resort principle rather than a detailed dual-eligible pharmacy claim procedure. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_8.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, pharmacies must submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage before submitting to Medicaid, and Medicaid is payer of last resort; this source is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 says NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before Medicaid and that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy guidance says NYS Medicaid providers "are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and that "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy also states it is "the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says "Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid" and that if issues are resolved, the pharmacy may resubmit to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with TPL. It also states that "Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the T..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also says the total Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that Medicare/Medicaid payment, if any, "must be accepted as payment in full." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid: policy says providers "are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and the manual says pharmacies must submit claims to "a Medicaid member’s other coverage ... before submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/last resort: policy states "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort," and the manual requires other coverage to be used before submitting to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is more specific for pharmacy operations, stating pharmacies have a "corresponding responsibility" to follow the plan’s requirements and may resubmit to Medicaid only after the claim is "properly adjudicated with the TPL," while the policy excerpt is broader and only says to bill applicable third parties before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt includes a general billing instruction that the pharmacy must bill "the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid," while the provider manual excerpt focuses on other coverage/TPL and Medicare/Medicaid payment limits rather than listing all possible billing parties. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid: policy says providers "are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and the manual says pharmacies must submit claims to "a Medicaid member’s other coverage ... before submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/last resort: policy states "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort," and the manual requires other coverage to be used before submitting to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is more specific for pharmacy operations, stating pharmacies have a "corresponding responsibility" to follow the plan’s requirements and may resubmit to Medicaid only after the claim is "properly adjudicated with the TPL," while the policy excerpt is broader and only says to bill applicable third parties before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt includes a general billing instruction that the pharmacy must bill "the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid," while the provider manual excerpt focuses on other coverage/TPL and Medicare/Medicaid payment limits rather than listing all possible billing parties. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provider manual is the more recent source overall for this issue and should guide current practice by default. The provider manual excerpt is partially truncated in the provided chunk, so the quoted sentence about bypassing TPL is incomplete; however, the grounded text still clearly shows the other-coverage-before-Medicaid rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and the manual requires other coverage to be used before Medicaid and only then resubmission to Medicaid after TPL adjudication [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about process and timing, stating pharmacies have a "corresponding responsibility" to follow "the plan’s requirements" and may "resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy update states the broader payer-of-last-resort rule without that resubmission detail [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The policy sources include broader reminders about provider responsibility to seek TPL enrollment and bill the "appropriate party" including "facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy claim submission to other coverage before Medicaid and accepted payment rules for Medicare/Medicaid claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_10.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
test_id_10.pm_ground_truth: For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
test_id_10.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

test_id_11.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy sources dated 2025-07-01 and earlier say Medicaid is payer of last resort and that dual-eligible members generally access Medicare first, but the provider manual adds a more specific payment rule that Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full and that Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance cannot be recovered from the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says that for duals, Medicaid is secondary to other coverage: "Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] The July 2025 policy update also states that the manual update includes "a revised end date for Medicare flexibilities and emphasis that duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond these Medicare-first / payer-of-last-resort rules. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]

If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for provider manual documents beyond this payment-in-full and no-recovery rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as primary and Medicaid as secondary for dual-eligible members: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and that "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]; the provider manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment, if any, "must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules or Medicare-based billing first: policy says the July 2025 update includes "emphasis that duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025], and the provider manual addresses Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance recovery limits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more explicit about co-payment responsibility because it states that "the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and that a provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts" from the member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not expressly address member co-payment recovery.

The policy excerpts focus on billing order and payer-of-last-resort status—"Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and Medicare must be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]—whereas the provider manual adds the specific payment consequence that no additional payment is due if Medicare payment exceeds the Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules or Medicare-based billing first: policy says the July 2025 update includes "emphasis that duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025], and the provider manual addresses Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance recovery limits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more explicit about co-payment responsibility because it states that "the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and that a provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts" from the member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not expressly address member co-payment recovery.

https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.

The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
