provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond these Medicare-first / payer-of-last-resort rules. test_id_11.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_11.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_11.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_12.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy excerpts dated 2025-07-01 and earlier say Medicaid is payer of last resort and that dual-eligible members generally access Medicare first, but the provider manual adds a specific payment rule that Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full and that Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance cannot be recovered from the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: Policy guidance says Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual eligible enrollees: "Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] The newest policy update also says "duals are subject to Medicare rules" and that the manual update applies to Medicaid-enrolled providers and MMC plans, but it does not add a specific co-payment recovery rule in the provided excerpt. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states: "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] This is the clearest grounded provider-manual statement on co-payment responsibility in the provided excerpts, and it is more specific than the policy excerpts. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as primary for dual-eligible members before Medicaid pays: policy says "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and the provider manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment is capped and accepted as payment in full. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources indicate Medicaid does not function as the first payer for dual-eligible members: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and the provider manual limits total Medicare/Medicaid payment and requires it be "accepted as payment in full." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual gives a specific co-payment/coinsurance rule: "must be accepted as payment in full" and the provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts" from the member, while the policy excerpts do not provide that specific recovery prohibition. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]

The policy excerpts are broader billing guidance focused on payer order and Medicare rules for duals, stating "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and "duals are subject to Medicare rules," whereas the provider manual is more operational and directly addresses payment acceptance and prohibited recovery of deductible/coinsurance. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources treat Medicare as primary for dual-eligible members before Medicaid pays: policy says "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and the provider manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment is capped and accepted as payment in full. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources indicate Medicaid does not function as the first payer for dual-eligible members: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and the provider manual limits total Medicare/Medicaid payment and requires it be "accepted as payment in full." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual gives a specific co-payment/coinsurance rule: "must be accepted as payment in full" and the provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts" from the member, while the policy excerpts do not provide that specific recovery prohibition. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]

The policy excerpts are broader billing guidance focused on payer order and Medicare rules for duals, stating "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and "duals are subject to Medicare rules," whereas the provider manual is more operational and directly addresses payment acceptance and prohibited recovery of deductible/coinsurance. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provider manual excerpt is truncated after "from Me...", so the exact full wording of the recovery prohibition is not fully visible in the provided chunk; however, the grounded text clearly states that Medicare/Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full and that Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance amounts cannot be recovered from the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_13.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy sources dated 2025-07-01 and earlier state that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules and that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid, but they do not specifically define co-payment responsibility in the provided excerpts. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says the Medicaid program updated the Telehealth Policy Manual on July 1, 2025 and that the manual applies to all NYS Medicaid-enrolled providers and MMC plans; it also notes “duals are subject to Medicare rules,” but it does not define co-payment responsibility. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance states that “Medicaid is the payer of last resort,” and that “potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid,” which addresses billing order rather than co-payment liability. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt states: “The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] This is the only grounded provider-manual evidence in the provided excerpts that directly addresses payment acceptance and recovery of Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance amounts, but the excerpt is truncated before completing the sentence. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources place dual-eligible members within Medicare-first or Medicare-governed billing/payment rules: policy says “duals are subject to Medicare rules,” and the provider manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment, if any, “must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources indicate Medicaid does not pay first for dual-eligible-related claims: policy says “Medicaid is the payer of last resort” and Medicare “must be billed prior to billing Medicaid,” while the provider manual limits total Medicare/Medicaid payment and bars additional payment when Medicare exceeds the Medicaid fee. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy excerpts do not specifically assign co-payment responsibility; they only say “duals are subject to Medicare rules” and that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]

The provider manual excerpt is more specific on payment responsibility, stating that “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full” and that a pharmacy provider “cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts” from the member, which is a direct co-payment/coinsurance recovery limitation not stated in the policy excerpts. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is newer than the policy excerpts (2025-10-01 vs. 2025-07-01 and 2023-02-01), so it should guide current operational handling of co-payment/coinsurance recovery unless a historical question is intended. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources place dual-eligible members within Medicare-first or Medicare-governed billing/payment rules: policy says “duals are subject to Medicare rules,” and the provider manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment, if any, “must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources indicate Medicaid does not pay first for dual-eligible-related claims: policy says “Medicaid is the payer of last resort” and Medicare “must be billed prior to billing Medicaid,” while the provider manual limits total Medicare/Medicaid payment and bars additional payment when Medicare exceeds the Medicaid fee. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy excerpts do not specifically assign co-payment responsibility; they only say “duals are subject to Medicare rules” and that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]

The provider manual excerpt is more specific on payment responsibility, stating that “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full” and that a pharmacy provider “cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts” from the member, which is a direct co-payment/coinsurance recovery limitation not stated in the policy excerpts. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is newer than the policy excerpts (2025-10-01 vs. 2025-07-01 and 2023-02-01), so it should guide current operational handling of co-payment/coinsurance recovery unless a historical question is intended. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_13.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_13.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_13.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_14.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy source dated 2025-07-01 says Medicaid is payer of last resort and Medicare must be billed first for dual enrollees, while the provider manual adds that Medicare/Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full and Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance cannot be recovered from the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says that for dual eligible enrollees, "Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted" and "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] The July 2025 policy update also states that the telehealth manual update includes "a revised end date for Medicare flexibilities and emphasis that duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence states that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] It further says, "Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and "a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from" the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources place Medicare ahead of Medicaid for dual-eligible billing/payment responsibility: policy says "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and the manual limits combined Medicare/Medicaid payment and treats it as payment in full. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources indicate dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules or Medicare-first processing: policy says "duals are subject to Medicare rules" and the manual addresses Medicare/Medicaid payment and Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance recovery. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy source states the general coordination-of-benefits rule only: "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and Medicare must be billed first, but it does not in the provided text address whether the member can be billed for deductible or coinsurance amounts. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]

The provider manual adds a specific member-protection/payment rule that "the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and that a provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from" the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources place Medicare ahead of Medicaid for dual-eligible billing/payment responsibility: policy says "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and the manual limits combined Medicare/Medicaid payment and treats it as payment in full. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources indicate dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules or Medicare-first processing: policy says "duals are subject to Medicare rules" and the manual addresses Medicare/Medicaid payment and Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance recovery. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy source states the general coordination-of-benefits rule only: "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and Medicare must be billed first, but it does not in the provided text address whether the member can be billed for deductible or coinsurance amounts. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]

The provider manual adds a specific member-protection/payment rule that "the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and that a provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from" the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not include a direct, current policy statement specifically addressing member co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members; the clearest policy evidence is the payer-of-last-resort/Medicare-first rule. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]

test_id_14.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_14.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_15.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy excerpts dated 2025-07-01 and earlier say dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules and that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid, but they do not specifically define co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says the Medicaid program updated the Telehealth Policy Manual on July 1, 2025 and that the update includes “a revised end date for Medicare flexibilities and emphasis that duals are subject to Medicare rules” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]. Earlier policy guidance states that “Medicaid is the payer of last resort,” and that “potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members for policy beyond these Medicare-first / duals-subject-to-Medicare-rules statements. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt says, “The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. This is the clearest current provider-manual guidance in the provided excerpts on payment responsibility, but the excerpt is truncated and does not fully spell out dual-eligible member co-payment responsibility. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources place Medicare ahead of Medicaid for dual-eligible situations: policy says “duals are subject to Medicare rules” and that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023].

Both sources indicate Medicaid does not pay beyond the Medicare-related amount in the cited pharmacy context: the provider manual says “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full,” while policy says Medicaid is the “payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023].
differences: The provider manual gives a more specific payment rule, stating that “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full” and that a provider “cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts” from the member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts do not specifically address co-payment responsibility; they only say duals are subject to Medicare rules and that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid, so there is insufficient grounded evidence in policy to match the provider manual’s explicit deductible/coinsurance language [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023].
comparison: Both sources place Medicare ahead of Medicaid for dual-eligible situations: policy says “duals are subject to Medicare rules” and that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023].

Both sources indicate Medicaid does not pay beyond the Medicare-related amount in the cited pharmacy context: the provider manual says “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full,” while policy says Medicaid is the “payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023].

The provider manual gives a more specific payment rule, stating that “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full” and that a provider “cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts” from the member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts do not specifically address co-payment responsibility; they only say duals are subject to Medicare rules and that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid, so there is insufficient grounded evidence in policy to match the provider manual’s explicit deductible/coinsurance language [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023].
answers.caveats: The provider manual excerpt is truncated at “from Me...,” so the quoted sentence on deductible/coinsurance is incomplete in the provided chunk. Also, the provided policy excerpts do not contain a direct co-payment rule for dual-eligible members, so any comparison on that exact point is limited to the Medicare-first and payment-in-full language shown here.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_16.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The provider manual says Medicaid pays only for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list, while the newest policy source dated 2023-05-01 says most items may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid but does not specifically define Medicare Part D-excluded drugs. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid recognizes certain drugs that require practitioner administration and may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy: "NYS Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy." [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023] It also says, more generally, "Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — Mar 01, 2021] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states: "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025] It then limits Medicaid payment to drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list where "the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources allow Medicaid billing in limited circumstances rather than as a blanket rule: the policy says "These items may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — Mar 01, 2021], and the provider manual says Medicaid pays only for certain excluded drugs "unless as stated below" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources preserve Medicaid as the payer only for specific categories of drugs, not all drugs: the policy references drugs that "may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023], while the provider manual limits payment to drugs "specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is explicit about Medicare Part D-excluded drugs and says Medicaid pays only for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is "specifically excluded by law" from Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts provided do not specifically define or address Medicare Part D-excluded drugs [Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023].

The policy excerpts focus on broader billing mechanics and practitioner-administered drugs—"may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023]—whereas the provider manual gives the current Part D exclusion rule and identifies examples such as "Select prescription vitamins" and "Certain non-prescription drugs" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources allow Medicaid billing in limited circumstances rather than as a blanket rule: the policy says "These items may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — Mar 01, 2021], and the provider manual says Medicaid pays only for certain excluded drugs "unless as stated below" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources preserve Medicaid as the payer only for specific categories of drugs, not all drugs: the policy references drugs that "may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023], while the provider manual limits payment to drugs "specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is explicit about Medicare Part D-excluded drugs and says Medicaid pays only for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is "specifically excluded by law" from Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts provided do not specifically define or address Medicare Part D-excluded drugs [Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023].

The policy excerpts focus on broader billing mechanics and practitioner-administered drugs—"may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023]—whereas the provider manual gives the current Part D exclusion rule and identifies examples such as "Select prescription vitamins" and "Certain non-prescription drugs" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a direct, grounded definition of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, so the policy side is incomplete for this specific concept. The provider manual is newer and should guide current handling of this issue by default.

policy.text:
Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items
may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid.
• The items in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics,
Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject
to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan.
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the carve-out, that require pre-approval. To ensure a
smooth transition for Medicaid MC member’s, PA requirements will be temporarily relaxed (e.g.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.

• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_16.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
