This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_17.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The provider manual says Medicaid pays only for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list, while the newest policy source dated 2023-05-01 addresses practitioner-administered drugs and does not specifically define Part D-excluded drug handling. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence in the provided excerpts does not specifically define how Medicaid handles Medicare Part D-excluded drugs; it instead states that NYS Medicaid recognizes certain drugs require practitioner administration and may be billed by either a practitioner or a pharmacy: "NYS Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy." [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states: "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025] It then limits Medicaid payment to drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list where "the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources address Medicaid billing for drugs in a limited way rather than as unrestricted coverage: the policy source says certain drugs "may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023], and the provider manual says it is "not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources are Medicaid-focused and concern how drugs are billed to NYS Medicaid, with the policy source referring to drugs that "may be billed to NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023] and the provider manual stating "Medicaid only pays" for certain Part D-excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific Part D-exclusion rule: Medicaid pays only for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is "specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt provided does not mention Part D exclusions at all and instead discusses practitioner-administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023].

The provider manual is newer and more directly controlling for this question, dated 2025-10-01 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the newest policy excerpt is dated 2023-05-01 and does not supply grounded evidence on Part D-excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023].
comparison: Both sources address Medicaid billing for drugs in a limited way rather than as unrestricted coverage: the policy source says certain drugs "may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023], and the provider manual says it is "not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources are Medicaid-focused and concern how drugs are billed to NYS Medicaid, with the policy source referring to drugs that "may be billed to NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023] and the provider manual stating "Medicaid only pays" for certain Part D-excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual gives a specific Part D-exclusion rule: Medicaid pays only for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is "specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt provided does not mention Part D exclusions at all and instead discusses practitioner-administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023].

The provider manual is newer and more directly controlling for this question, dated 2025-10-01 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the newest policy excerpt is dated 2023-05-01 and does not supply grounded evidence on Part D-excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a direct Part D-excluded-drug rule, so the policy side cannot be fully compared on that exact concept from the grounded text alone. The provider manual is the more recent source for this issue and should guide current handling unless a different, more specific policy source is provided.

policy.text:
Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items
may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid.
• The items in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics,
Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject
to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan.
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the carve-out, that require pre-approval. To ensure a
smooth transition for Medicaid MC member’s, PA requirements will be temporarily relaxed (e.g.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items
may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid.
• The items in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics,
Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject
to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan.
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the carve-out, that require pre-approval. To ensure a
smooth transition for Medicaid MC member’s, PA requirements will be temporarily relaxed (e.g.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_18.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_18.pm_ground_truth: Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_18.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_19.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The provider manual says Medicaid pays only for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list, while the newest policy source dated 2023-05-01 says most items may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid and does not specifically define Part D-excluded drugs. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says, "Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — Mar 01, 2021] The newest policy source also says, "Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner" and that NYS Medicaid recognizes certain drugs may be billed by either a practitioner or a pharmacy, but it does not specifically define how Medicare Part D-excluded drugs are handled. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says, "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025] It further states, "Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources support direct Medicaid billing in some circumstances, but only within their stated limits: policy says items "may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — Mar 01, 2021], and the provider manual says Medicaid pays only for certain Part D-excluded drugs on the reimbursable list [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources show that Medicaid payment is not automatic for all drugs; each source describes a restricted billing/payment rule rather than universal coverage [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — Mar 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part D members and says, "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts provided do not specifically define Medicare Part D-excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023].

The provider manual gives a narrow exception tied to drugs "specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans" and limited to the "List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the policy excerpt says only that "Most items" may be billed directly to Medicaid and does not provide the same Part D-exclusion framework [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — Mar 01, 2021].
comparison: Both sources support direct Medicaid billing in some circumstances, but only within their stated limits: policy says items "may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — Mar 01, 2021], and the provider manual says Medicaid pays only for certain Part D-excluded drugs on the reimbursable list [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources show that Medicaid payment is not automatic for all drugs; each source describes a restricted billing/payment rule rather than universal coverage [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — Mar 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part D members and says, "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts provided do not specifically define Medicare Part D-excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023].

The provider manual gives a narrow exception tied to drugs "specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans" and limited to the "List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the policy excerpt says only that "Most items" may be billed directly to Medicaid and does not provide the same Part D-exclusion framework [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — Mar 01, 2021].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a grounded, specific definition of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, so the policy side is incomplete for this exact concept. The provider manual is newer and should guide current handling of this issue by default.

This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_20.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2023-05-01 is consistent in direction but less specific, while the provider manual expressly limits Medicaid billing for Medicare Part D members to drugs that are excluded from Part D by law.
answers.policy_definition: Policy guidance says Medicaid billing is generally not appropriate when Medicare is involved unless the drug is not covered by the primary insurer: “Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] The newest policy excerpt also states that some drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid and that certain DME items are excluded from the carve-out, but it does not specifically define Medicare Part D-excluded drugs in the provided text. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — Mar 01, 2021]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance is explicit: “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025] It then limits Medicaid payment to drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list “where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require that Medicaid not be billed broadly for drugs when another payer framework applies, and both preserve Medicaid payment only in limited circumstances. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources support Medicaid payment only when the drug is outside the other payer’s coverage scope, with the provider manual specifying Part D-law exclusions and the policy excerpt stating that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is more specific and current: it expressly addresses “a Medicare Part D member” and says Medicaid pays only for drugs “specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpt does not specifically define Medicare Part D-excluded drugs in the provided text. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — Mar 01, 2021]

The policy excerpt is broader and third-party oriented, focusing on resolving claim issues with the primary insurer before Medicaid submission and denying claims when the primary insurer does not cover the medication, rather than naming Part D exclusions. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] The provider manual instead gives a direct billing rule for Medicare Part D members and identifies examples of excluded drug types. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require that Medicaid not be billed broadly for drugs when another payer framework applies, and both preserve Medicaid payment only in limited circumstances. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources support Medicaid payment only when the drug is outside the other payer’s coverage scope, with the provider manual specifying Part D-law exclusions and the policy excerpt stating that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is more specific and current: it expressly addresses “a Medicare Part D member” and says Medicaid pays only for drugs “specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpt does not specifically define Medicare Part D-excluded drugs in the provided text. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — Mar 01, 2021]

The policy excerpt is broader and third-party oriented, focusing on resolving claim issues with the primary insurer before Medicaid submission and denying claims when the primary insurer does not cover the medication, rather than naming Part D exclusions. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] The provider manual instead gives a direct billing rule for Medicare Part D members and identifies examples of excluded drug types. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond the general third-party billing rule and the 2021 carve-out excerpt. The provider manual is the controlling current guidance for this issue based on the newer 2025 effective date.

policy.text:
Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items
may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid.
• The items in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics,
Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject
to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan.
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the carve-out, that require pre-approval. To ensure a
smooth transition for Medicaid MC member’s, PA requirements will be temporarily relaxed (e.g.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_21.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources require Medicaid to be billed only after other liable coverage is billed first, but the provider manual adds a specific Medicare rule for pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid, because Medicaid is the payer of last resort: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]. The older policy reminder also says pharmacies must bill "the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid" and that it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that pharmacies enrolled in Medicaid as billing providers "are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and that "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid"; it further says that "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also says pharmacies must submit claims to a member’s other coverage "per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be billed before Medicaid: policy says providers must bill "applicable third parties" before Medicaid, and the manual says claims must be submitted to "other coverage" before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available resources: policy says "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort," and the manual says "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about Medicare: it says pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers "are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and that services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid "must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. The policy excerpt only says to bill applicable third parties before Medicaid and does not separately spell out this Medicare-first rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts are broader and less detailed, referring generally to "applicable third parties" and "the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid," while the provider manual gives a pharmacy-specific billing sequence tied to Medicare participation and dual-covered services [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require other coverage to be billed before Medicaid: policy says providers must bill "applicable third parties" before Medicaid, and the manual says claims must be submitted to "other coverage" before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available resources: policy says "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort," and the manual says "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more specific about Medicare: it says pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers "are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and that services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid "must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. The policy excerpt only says to bill applicable third parties before Medicaid and does not separately spell out this Medicare-first rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts are broader and less detailed, referring generally to "applicable third parties" and "the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid," while the provider manual gives a pharmacy-specific billing sequence tied to Medicare participation and dual-covered services [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The newest provider manual source is more recent than the newest policy source, so it should guide current pharmacy billing practice for this issue unless you are analyzing a historical period. The policy excerpts do not provide a separate, explicit Medicare-first rule for pharmacies; they only establish the general third-party-before-Medicaid requirement.

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

test_id_21.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_21.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_21.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_22.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_22.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. It says pharmacies enrolled as billing providers must demonstrate Medicare participation and that Medicare benefits must be maximized before billing Medicaid, while the newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 says NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before billing Medicaid: "New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Older policy language is consistent: "the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual source says pharmacies enrolled as billing providers must participate in Medicare and must bill Medicare before Medicaid when both cover the service: "Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program... Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says pharmacies must submit claims to other coverage before Medicaid: "Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require billing other coverage before Medicaid: "required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and "submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage... before submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available coverage: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about Medicare: "Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. The policy update is broader and refers to "applicable third parties" without the same Medicare-specific billing rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The provider manual ties the rule to pharmacies enrolled as billing providers and to services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy update states the general Medicaid rule that providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require billing other coverage before Medicaid: "required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and "submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage... before submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available coverage: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more specific about Medicare: "Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. The policy update is broader and refers to "applicable third parties" without the same Medicare-specific billing rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The provider manual ties the rule to pharmacies enrolled as billing providers and to services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy update states the general Medicaid rule that providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provider manual is the newer and more specific source for current pharmacy billing guidance on Medicare-before-Medicaid. The policy excerpts are consistent but broader; the 2020 policy excerpt is background only and does not add a separate Medicare-before-Medicaid rule beyond timely billing to the appropriate party [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020].

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
