answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a detailed Medicare-first rule as explicit as the provider manual, so the policy side is broader and less specific on this exact question. The newest provider manual source is later than the newest policy source, so it should guide current billing practice unless the user is asking about a historical period. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_23.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_23.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_23.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_24.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_24.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources require Medicaid to be billed only after other liable coverage is billed first, but the provider manual specifically says Medicare benefits must be maximized before billing Medicaid and that services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties that may be liable before billing NYS Medicaid, because NYS Medicaid is the payor of last resort and all other available resources must be used before Medicaid considers payment. The 2025 policy update repeats this same coordination-of-benefits rule for third parties generally, and the 2019/2020 pharmacy reminders add that pharmacies must bill the appropriate party, including Medicare or Medicaid, but do not separately define a Medicare-first rule. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers are required to demonstrate participation in Medicare, and that Medicare benefits must be maximized before billing Medicaid; it further states that services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. The manual also says pharmacies must submit claims to a member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid: policy says providers must bill "applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025], and the manual says pharmacies must submit claims to a member’s other coverage before submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available coverage: policy says "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025], and the manual says "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about Medicare: it says pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers "are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and that "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts only say to bill applicable third parties before Medicaid and do not separately state a Medicare-first rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts frame the rule broadly as a coordination-of-benefits / payer-of-last-resort requirement for all liable third parties [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025], whereas the provider manual adds pharmacy-enrollment and Medicare-participation requirements tied to billing Medicaid as a pharmacy billing provider [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid: policy says providers must bill "applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025], and the manual says pharmacies must submit claims to a member’s other coverage before submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available coverage: policy says "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025], and the manual says "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more specific about Medicare: it says pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers "are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and that "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts only say to bill applicable third parties before Medicaid and do not separately state a Medicare-first rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts frame the rule broadly as a coordination-of-benefits / payer-of-last-resort requirement for all liable third parties [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025], whereas the provider manual adds pharmacy-enrollment and Medicare-participation requirements tied to billing Medicaid as a pharmacy billing provider [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provider manual is newer than the policy excerpts for this issue, so it should guide current practice by default. The policy excerpts do not provide a separate, explicit Medicare-before-Medicaid rule; they only support the broader third-party-before-Medicaid requirement. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]

Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts only say to bill applicable third parties before Medicaid and do not separately state a Medicare-first rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025].", "The policy excerpts frame the rule broadly as a coordination-of-benefits / payer-of-last-resort requirement for all liable third parties [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025], whereas the provider manual adds pharmacy-enrollment and Medicare-participation requirements tied to billing Medicaid as a pharmacy billing provider [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "caveats": "The provider manual is newer than the policy excerpts for this issue, so it should guide current practice by default. The policy excerpts do not provide a separate, explicit Medicare-before-Medicaid rule; they only support the broader third-party-before-Medicaid requirement. test_id_24.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_24.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_24.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_25.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_25.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. It says pharmacies enrolled as billing providers must demonstrate Medicare participation and that Medicare benefits must be maximized before billing Medicaid, while the newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 says providers must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source states that "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and that "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" because "all other available resources" must be used first. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] The older policy reminder also says it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill "the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid," but it does not separately define a Medicare-before-Medicaid rule beyond that general billing responsibility. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual source states that "Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and that "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid." It further says that "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also says pharmacies must submit claims to a member’s other coverage "per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require billing other available coverage before Medicaid: the policy says providers must bill "applicable third parties" before NYS Medicaid, and the manual says "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/last resort: the policy says "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort," and the manual says Medicare must be maximized before billing Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is more specific about Medicare, stating that pharmacies enrolled as billing providers "are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and that services covered by both programs "must first be billed to Medicare," while the policy excerpt only states a general third-party-before-Medicaid rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt frames the rule broadly as billing "applicable third parties" and using "all other available resources" before Medicaid, whereas the manual ties the rule to pharmacy enrollment and Medicare participation requirements. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require billing other available coverage before Medicaid: the policy says providers must bill "applicable third parties" before NYS Medicaid, and the manual says "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/last resort: the policy says "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort," and the manual says Medicare must be maximized before billing Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is more specific about Medicare, stating that pharmacies enrolled as billing providers "are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and that services covered by both programs "must first be billed to Medicare," while the policy excerpt only states a general third-party-before-Medicaid rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt frames the rule broadly as billing "applicable third parties" and using "all other available resources" before Medicaid, whereas the manual ties the rule to pharmacy enrollment and Medicare participation requirements. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a detailed Medicare-specific pharmacy billing rule comparable to the provider manual’s explicit instruction, so the policy side is broader and less specific on this exact question. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_26.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_26.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the current guidance is that if a prescription/order requires prior approval or prior authorization, the provider must enter the assigned prior approval/authorization number on the claim; this provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2021-11-01 is consistent on the broader third-party-liability rule that all available resources must be used before Medicaid payment, but it does not specifically define crossover-claim prior approval after Medicaid payment.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says providers must pursue other coverage before Medicaid payment: “Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first.” [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021] Earlier policy guidance also says “Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence says: “If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025] The provider manual also states the broader third-party-liability rule: “Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define specific crossover-claim prior approval requirements after Medicaid payment for provider manual documents.
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be used before Medicaid payment: “all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment” and the provider must “pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie claim submission to prior authorization/prior approval handling when applicable: policy says “Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid,” and the provider manual says to “enter the prior approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is newer and more specific about claim entry requirements: “enter the prior approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order,” while the policy excerpt only states that third-party PA issues must be resolved before Medicaid submission. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]

The policy excerpts do not specifically address crossover claims or post-Medicaid-payment prior approval requirements, whereas the provider manual excerpts also do not specifically define crossover claims; both are limited to general third-party liability / prior approval language. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require other coverage to be used before Medicaid payment: “all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment” and the provider must “pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie claim submission to prior authorization/prior approval handling when applicable: policy says “Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid,” and the provider manual says to “enter the prior approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025]

The provider manual is newer and more specific about claim entry requirements: “enter the prior approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order,” while the policy excerpt only states that third-party PA issues must be resolved before Medicaid submission. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]

The policy excerpts do not specifically address crossover claims or post-Medicaid-payment prior approval requirements, whereas the provider manual excerpts also do not specifically define crossover claims; both are limited to general third-party liability / prior approval language. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided excerpts do not contain a direct definition of “crossover claims” or a specific rule for “prior approval after Medicaid payment.” The most current grounded guidance is the provider manual dated 2025-10-01, but it addresses prior approval/authorization generally rather than crossover claims specifically.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:
Edit# Edit Description NCPDP Reject Reject Message Notes/Resolutions
Code
Item Not Eligible For Product not on
00551 MR
Payment On Fill Date formulary
PA Required Call Prime
Therapeutics State
Government Solutions. This is
specific to topical compounds.
To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior
authorization clinical call
Compound Claim Prior Authorization center at 1-877-309-9493.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.

test_id_26.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_26.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_26.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_27.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_27.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. For crossover claims, the current manual says the responsible third party must pay first and that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome that responsibility, while the newest policy source dated 2021-11-01 says providers must pursue available other coverage before Medicaid considers payment. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says Medicaid is payer of last resort and providers must pursue available other coverage before submitting a claim: "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible th..." [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021]. Earlier policy guidance also says "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence says Medicaid requires other coverage to be used first and that prior authorization does not override that rule: "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit ..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]. The manual also separately states that if billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, the provider must enter the assigned prior approval/authorization number on the claim. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be pursued before Medicaid payment: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] and "a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie prior authorization/prior approval to claim processing, with the policy saying "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] and the manual saying "If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior approval/authorization number assigned" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025].
differences: The newest provider manual is more explicit that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit ..." of the responsible third party to pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not state that specific exception in the provided text [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021].

The policy excerpts specifically mention attaching an Explanation of Benefits from third-party payors to Medicaid claims: "providers will be required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (E..." [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020], but no comparable EOB-attachment requirement appears in the provided provider manual excerpts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require other coverage to be pursued before Medicaid payment: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] and "a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie prior authorization/prior approval to claim processing, with the policy saying "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] and the manual saying "If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior approval/authorization number assigned" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025].

The newest provider manual is more explicit that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit ..." of the responsible third party to pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not state that specific exception in the provided text [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021].

The policy excerpts specifically mention attaching an Explanation of Benefits from third-party payors to Medicaid claims: "providers will be required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (E..." [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020], but no comparable EOB-attachment requirement appears in the provided provider manual excerpts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided excerpts do not include a full, explicit crossover-claims prior-approval rule after Medicaid payment; they mainly address third-party billing first, prior authorization/approval handling, and claim documentation. The policy excerpt on EOB attachment is truncated, so its full scope cannot be confirmed from the provided text.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

test_id_27.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
