test_id_28.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_28.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. For crossover claims, the grounded text says Medicaid is the payor of last resort and any available other coverage must be pursued first; the policy source also says any claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, must be resolved with the third party before submitting to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says that for claims with other coverage, "all available resources [must] be used before Medicaid considers payment" and a provider "must pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid"; if there is a responsible third party, "that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021] Earlier policy guidance also states: "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence says: "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid." It also states that if a responsible third party should be paying as primary payor, "that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] The provided provider manual chunks do not specifically define a separate post-Medicaid-payment prior approval rule for crossover claims beyond this third-party-liability requirement. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be used before Medicaid payment: policy says "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and the provider manual says the same. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources require the provider to pursue other coverage before submitting the claim to Medicaid: policy says "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid," and the provider manual says the same. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy source is more explicit about prior authorization issues, stating that "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]

The provider manual excerpt provided does not specifically mention resolving prior authorization requirements with the third party before Medicaid submission; it only states that other coverage must be pursued first and the responsible third party must pay first. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require other coverage to be used before Medicaid payment: policy says "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and the provider manual says the same. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources require the provider to pursue other coverage before submitting the claim to Medicaid: policy says "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid," and the provider manual says the same. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy source is more explicit about prior authorization issues, stating that "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]

The provider manual excerpt provided does not specifically mention resolving prior authorization requirements with the third party before Medicaid submission; it only states that other coverage must be pursued first and the responsible third party must pay first. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define a distinct "prior approval after Medicaid payment" rule for crossover claims beyond the third-party-liability and prior-authorization-before-submission language quoted above. The provided chunks do not show a specific post-payment prior approval process for crossover claims.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 15

Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients
with Other Coverage
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to
submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid
member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. As previously
communicated in the October 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled Notification to Providers
of Requirement to Attach Explanation of Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to
obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will deny at point of service with National Council for Prescription
Drug Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items
may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid.
• The items in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics,
Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject
to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan.
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the carve-out, that require pre-approval. To ensure a
smooth transition for Medicaid MC member’s, PA requirements will be temporarily relaxed (e.g.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:
Edit# Edit Description NCPDP Reject Reject Message Notes/Resolutions
Code
Item Not Eligible For Product not on
00551 MR
Payment On Fill Date formulary
PA Required Call Prime
Therapeutics State
Government Solutions. This is
specific to topical compounds.
To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior
authorization clinical call
Compound Claim Prior Authorization center at 1-877-309-9493.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 15

Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients
with Other Coverage
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to
submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid
member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. As previously
communicated in the October 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled Notification to Providers
of Requirement to Attach Explanation of Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to
obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will deny at point of service with National Council for Prescription
Drug Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”.

Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject
to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan.
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the carve-out, that require pre-approval. To ensure a

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:
Edit# Edit Description NCPDP Reject Reject Message Notes/Resolutions
Code
Item Not Eligible For Product not on
00551 MR
Payment On Fill Date formulary
PA Required Call Prime
Therapeutics State
Government Solutions. This is
specific to topical compounds.
To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior
authorization clinical call
Compound Claim Prior Authorization center at 1-877-309-9493.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_29.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_29.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_29.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_30.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_30.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the current guidance is that if a claim requires prior approval/authorization, the provider must enter the assigned prior approval/authorization number on the claim; this provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2021-11-01 is consistent that other coverage must be pursued first, but it does not provide a crossover-claim-specific post-Medicaid-payment prior approval rule in the provided excerpts.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says providers must pursue other coverage before Medicaid payment: "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021] It also says, "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents as a crossover-claim-specific requirement after Medicaid payment.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt states that when a prescription/order requires prior approval or prior authorization, the provider must report it on the claim: "If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025] The policy guidelines also state that other coverage must be used first: "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for provider manual documents as a crossover-claim-specific requirement after Medicaid payment.
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be pursued before Medicaid payment: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] and "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie claim processing to prior authorization/prior approval when applicable: "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] and "If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is newer and more specific about claim entry: "enter the prior approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025], while the policy excerpts only say third-party PA issues must be resolved before Medicaid submission [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020].

The policy excerpts focus on billing the primary payer first and attaching EOBs: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020], whereas the provider manual excerpts emphasize general third-party liability and claim-field reporting of prior approval numbers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require other coverage to be pursued before Medicaid payment: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] and "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie claim processing to prior authorization/prior approval when applicable: "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] and "If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025].

The provider manual is newer and more specific about claim entry: "enter the prior approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025], while the policy excerpts only say third-party PA issues must be resolved before Medicaid submission [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020].

The policy excerpts focus on billing the primary payer first and attaching EOBs: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020], whereas the provider manual excerpts emphasize general third-party liability and claim-field reporting of prior approval numbers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15-16 — Jul 23, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided excerpts do not contain a direct crossover-claim-specific rule stating what prior approval is required after Medicaid payment; the grounded evidence only supports pre-submission third-party billing/PA handling and claim reporting of prior approval numbers. The provider manual is the more recent source overall for this issue and should guide current practice unless a more specific crossover-claim policy is provided.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.

approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.

test_id_30.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_30.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_30.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_31.question: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for COB and should receive more weight by default. The policy sources dated 2025-04-01 and earlier say Medicaid is the payer of last resort and other liable third parties must be billed first, but the provider manual adds a specific Workers’ Compensation rule and billing exception.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents define Coordination of Benefits (COB) as requiring that the correct party pay first: “Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] They state that “NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment,” and that “the responsible third-party should pay first.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] The newest policy update also says “NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” and repeats that “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states a specific COB rule for Workers’ Compensation: “If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] It also provides an exception: “If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of ‘2’ in field 309-C9.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other available coverage to be used before NYS Medicaid/NYRx pays: “all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] and “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid/NYRx as secondary or last-resort coverage: “NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] and “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].
differences: The policy documents state the general COB rule for all claims—“the correct party pays first” and “the responsible third-party should pay first” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022]—while the provider manual gives a narrower, pharmacy-specific Workers’ Compensation rule and exception [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual adds an explicit billing exception not stated in the policy excerpts: if the claim is unrelated to the Workers’ Compensation case, “NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of ‘2’” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require other available coverage to be used before NYS Medicaid/NYRx pays: “all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] and “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid/NYRx as secondary or last-resort coverage: “NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] and “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The policy documents state the general COB rule for all claims—“the correct party pays first” and “the responsible third-party should pay first” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022]—while the provider manual gives a narrower, pharmacy-specific Workers’ Compensation rule and exception [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual adds an explicit billing exception not stated in the policy excerpts: if the claim is unrelated to the Workers’ Compensation case, “NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of ‘2’” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond the general last-resort/third-party-first rule, and insufficient grounded evidence to define COB comprehensively for the provider manual beyond the Workers’ Compensation pharmacy billing rule and exception. The newest provider manual source is more recent than the newest policy source, so it should guide current pharmacy COB billing questions unless the user is asking about historical policy.

policy.text:
Additional information regarding Coordination
of Benefits (COB) can be found in the following Medicaid Update articles:
• Update on Coordination of Benefits Submissions for Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing article
published in the October 2022 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://
www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/docs/mu_no12_oct22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits article published in the April 2022 issue of the

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note:
• Outpatient dispensing, community-based, open-door pharmacies applying for enrollment in COS 0441
must be full service. This is defined as dispensing and maintaining an inventory of commonly dispensed
outpatient prescription medications, as well as providing diabetic supplies and immunizations. Community
pharmacies that serve a particular diagnosis, situation, or niche or that do not immunize do not meet COS
0441 enrollment criteria.
• Pharmacies that are embedded or located (not separate and distinct) within a healthcare facility, physician’s
office, or group, or in a hospital setting, are not classified or enrollable under COS 0441 (see clinic and
hospital pharmacy COS below), this includes pharmacies with pass through windows or doors, or situated
where patients have to walk through a healthcare facility, office area, or otherwise to reach the pharmacy or
those that share a waiting area.
• Pharmacies whose physical registered space is not separate and distinct from other unaffiliated or
separately operated retail or medical entities do not qualify for pharmacy enrollment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_31.ground_truth:
"It is the pharmacy's duty to make ""reasonable efforts"" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission

The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP ""Other Payer"" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail."

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission
test_id_31.policy_ground_truth: The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP "Other Payer" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail.

Both sources place Medicaid in a secondary position when another payer is responsible: “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] and “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note:
• Outpatient dispensing, community-based, open-door pharmacies applying for enrollment in COS 0441
must be full service. This is defined as dispensing and maintaining an inventory of commonly dispensed
outpatient prescription medications, as well as providing diabetic supplies and immunizations. Community
pharmacies that serve a particular diagnosis, situation, or niche or that do not immunize do not meet COS
0441 enrollment criteria.
• Pharmacies that are embedded or located (not separate and distinct) within a healthcare facility, physician’s
office, or group, or in a hospital setting, are not classified or enrollable under COS 0441 (see clinic and
hospital pharmacy COS below), this includes pharmacies with pass through windows or doors, or situated
where patients have to walk through a healthcare facility, office area, or otherwise to reach the pharmacy or
those that share a waiting area.
• Pharmacies whose physical registered space is not separate and distinct from other unaffiliated or
separately operated retail or medical entities do not qualify for pharmacy enrollment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_32.ground_truth:
"It is the pharmacy's duty to make ""reasonable efforts"" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission

The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP ""Other Payer"" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail."

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission
test_id_32.policy_ground_truth: The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP "Other Payer" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail.

test_id_33.question: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy sources dated 2025-04-01 and earlier say COB means other liable coverage must be billed first because NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, but the provider manual adds a specific Workers’ Compensation rule.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy guidance says NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid: “NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance states that “Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first” and that “NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says that for a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.” It also says that if the drug claim is not related to the Workers’ Compensation case, “NYRx may be billed as the primary payer” with Eligibility Clarification Code “2” in field 309-C9. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be used before Medicaid: “NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” and “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] / [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources frame COB as a first-payer/last-resort rule: “Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first” and “NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] / [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual adds a specific Workers’ Compensation requirement that is not stated in the policy excerpts: “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy excerpts describe COB more generally as third-party-first / Medicaid-last-resort billing, while the provider manual excerpt gives a narrower operational rule for work-related injuries and an exception when the claim is unrelated to the Workers’ Compensation case. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] / [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require other liable coverage to be used before Medicaid: “NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” and “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] / [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources frame COB as a first-payer/last-resort rule: “Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first” and “NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] / [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]

The provider manual adds a specific Workers’ Compensation requirement that is not stated in the policy excerpts: “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy excerpts describe COB more generally as third-party-first / Medicaid-last-resort billing, while the provider manual excerpt gives a narrower operational rule for work-related injuries and an exception when the claim is unrelated to the Workers’ Compensation case. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] / [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for the policy source beyond the general third-party-first / payer-of-last-resort rule. The provider manual excerpt is more recent and should guide current pharmacy billing practice for the specific Workers’ Compensation scenario, but the provided chunks do not include a full COB definition or all possible exceptions.

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents define COB as requiring the correct party to pay first: “Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — Oct 01, 2022] They state that “NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment,” and that if there is a responsible third party, “the responsible third-party should pay first.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — Oct 01, 2022] The April 2025 update likewise says providers “are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” and that “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that for workers’ compensation, “If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] It also provides an exception: “If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of ‘2’ in field 309-C9.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be used before NYS Medicaid: “NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — Oct 01, 2022] and “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” with providers required to bill liable third parties first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025].

Both sources place primary responsibility on the liable third party before Medicaid: “the responsible third-party should pay first” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — Oct 01, 2022] and “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more specific and operational for workers’ compensation claims, requiring prior use of workers’ compensation coverage and allowing NYRx to be billed as primary only when the claim is not related to the workers’ compensation case with Eligibility Clarification Code “2” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy updates are broader COB statements and do not provide the workers’ compensation exception or billing-code instruction found in the provider manual [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — Oct 01, 2022; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require other coverage to be used before NYS Medicaid: “NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — Oct 01, 2022] and “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” with providers required to bill liable third parties first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025].

Both sources place primary responsibility on the liable third party before Medicaid: “the responsible third-party should pay first” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — Oct 01, 2022] and “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more specific and operational for workers’ compensation claims, requiring prior use of workers’ compensation coverage and allowing NYRx to be billed as primary only when the claim is not related to the workers’ compensation case with Eligibility Clarification Code “2” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy updates are broader COB statements and do not provide the workers’ compensation exception or billing-code instruction found in the provider manual [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — Oct 01, 2022; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond the general last-resort/third-party-first rule; the provided policy excerpts do not include the full operational billing instructions. The provider manual excerpt is limited to workers’ compensation-related COB and does not define COB generally beyond that context.

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health
