provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note:
• Outpatient dispensing, community-based, open-door pharmacies applying for enrollment in COS 0441
must be full service. This is defined as dispensing and maintaining an inventory of commonly dispensed
outpatient prescription medications, as well as providing diabetic supplies and immunizations. Community
pharmacies that serve a particular diagnosis, situation, or niche or that do not immunize do not meet COS
0441 enrollment criteria.
• Pharmacies that are embedded or located (not separate and distinct) within a healthcare facility, physician’s
office, or group, or in a hospital setting, are not classified or enrollable under COS 0441 (see clinic and
hospital pharmacy COS below), this includes pharmacies with pass through windows or doors, or situated
where patients have to walk through a healthcare facility, office area, or otherwise to reach the pharmacy or
those that share a waiting area.
• Pharmacies whose physical registered space is not separate and distinct from other unaffiliated or
separately operated retail or medical entities do not qualify for pharmacy enrollment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_34.ground_truth:
"It is the pharmacy's duty to make ""reasonable efforts"" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission

The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP ""Other Payer"" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail."
test_id_34.pm_ground_truth:
It is the pharmacy's duty to make "reasonable efforts" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission
test_id_34.policy_ground_truth: The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP "Other Payer" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail.

test_id_35.ground_truth:
"It is the pharmacy's duty to make ""reasonable efforts"" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission

The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP ""Other Payer"" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail."

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission
test_id_35.policy_ground_truth: The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP "Other Payer" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail.

test_id_36.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_36.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The provider manual addresses missing member identifiers and says the pharmacist is not required to enter missing address, age, or CIN if certain conditions are met, while the newest policy source dated 2025-01-01 does not specifically define pharmacist duties for missing patient or prescriber information. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says the pharmacy must use professional judgment in a specific emergency-department scenario and contact the assigned PCP the next business day, and it separately says providers should verify prescription directions and consult the prescriber as necessary on changes: "The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient." [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025] "Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes." [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond these points.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence says that when the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter those items if either they are already readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate: "If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription if the information: • Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled, or • The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for provider manual documents beyond this missing-member-information rule.
similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist/pharmacy to exercise judgment or verification before dispensing: "The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions..." [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025] and "the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources allow action without complete information in limited circumstances rather than requiring automatic refusal: the policy source allows filling when the assigned PCP is unavailable after hours in an emergency-department context [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025], and the provider manual says missing address, age, or CIN need not be entered if the stated conditions are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about missing patient information, limiting the rule to missing "address, age, or CIN" and stating the pharmacist is "not required to enter any of these items" if the conditions are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy source does not give a comparable missing-information rule.

The policy source focuses on prescriber verification and follow-up in an emergency-department scenario—"contacting the assigned PCP the next business day" and verifying prescriptions when the PCP is unavailable [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]—whereas the provider manual focuses on whether missing member identifiers must be entered and does not mention next-business-day PCP contact [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist/pharmacy to exercise judgment or verification before dispensing: "The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions..." [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025] and "the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources allow action without complete information in limited circumstances rather than requiring automatic refusal: the policy source allows filling when the assigned PCP is unavailable after hours in an emergency-department context [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025], and the provider manual says missing address, age, or CIN need not be entered if the stated conditions are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more specific about missing patient information, limiting the rule to missing "address, age, or CIN" and stating the pharmacist is "not required to enter any of these items" if the conditions are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy source does not give a comparable missing-information rule.

The policy source focuses on prescriber verification and follow-up in an emergency-department scenario—"contacting the assigned PCP the next business day" and verifying prescriptions when the PCP is unavailable [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]—whereas the provider manual focuses on whether missing member identifiers must be entered and does not mention next-business-day PCP contact [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a direct, general rule for what pharmacists must do when required patient or prescriber information is missing, so the policy-side answer is limited to the emergency-department/PCP-verification language and the general direction to verify directions and consult the prescriber as necessary. The provider manual excerpt is limited to missing address, age, or CIN and does not address all possible missing patient or prescriber information.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_36.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.
test_id_36.policy_ground_truth: The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice.

for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_37.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

test_id_38.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_38.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. It says the pharmacist may not need to enter missing member address, age, or CIN if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate; the newest policy source dated 2025-01-01 instead addresses professional judgment and prescriber contact in other contexts, not this missing-information rule.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says: “The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025] Earlier policy also states: “Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes.” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence says: “If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription if the information: • Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled, or • The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025] It also states: “The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27-28 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist/pharmacy to use judgment or verify information rather than automatically filling without review: “The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement...” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025] and “the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources require follow-up/verification with another party when needed: “The pharmacy is responsible for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day...” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025] and “make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27-28 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual directly addresses missing member information and says the pharmacist is “not required to enter any of these items” when the stated conditions are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt does not define a missing-address/age/CIN rule and instead discusses emergency department prescriptions and PCP verification [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025].

The policy excerpt focuses on contacting the assigned PCP the next business day for emergency department prescriptions when the PCP is unavailable [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025], while the provider manual focuses on whether missing member data must be entered and on verifying prescriber identity when the prescriber is unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5;27-28 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist/pharmacy to use judgment or verify information rather than automatically filling without review: “The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement...” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025] and “the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources require follow-up/verification with another party when needed: “The pharmacy is responsible for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day...” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025] and “make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27-28 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual directly addresses missing member information and says the pharmacist is “not required to enter any of these items” when the stated conditions are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt does not define a missing-address/age/CIN rule and instead discusses emergency department prescriptions and PCP verification [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025].

The policy excerpt focuses on contacting the assigned PCP the next business day for emergency department prescriptions when the PCP is unavailable [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025], while the provider manual focuses on whether missing member data must be entered and on verifying prescriber identity when the prescriber is unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5;27-28 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a direct rule about what to do when required patient information such as address, age, or CIN is missing, so the policy-side answer is incomplete for that specific question. The provider manual is the more recent source for this issue and should guide current practice unless a different historical period is intended.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacist’s responsibility when required patient information is missing is limited to not needing to enter the member’s address, age, or CIN if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. The newest policy source dated 2025-01-01 is less specific on missing patient/prescriber information, so the provider manual should guide current practice for this issue by default. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says the pharmacy must use professional judgment in a specific emergency-department scenario: “The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions.” It also says, “The pharmacy is responsible for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025] Earlier policy material is broader but not specific to missing patient or prescriber information: “Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes.” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence states: “If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription if the information: • Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled, or • The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025] It also adds that controlled substances must still meet Article 33 requirements: “Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Hea...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist/pharmacy to use professional judgment rather than automatically reject a prescription when verification is needed or information is incomplete: “The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement...” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025] and “the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources preserve a verification/legitimacy check before dispensing: the policy says the pharmacy must be able to “verify the prescriptions” with the assigned PCP when available [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025], and the manual allows dispensing only if the pharmacist is “otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is directly about missing member information and says the pharmacist is “not required to enter” missing address, age, or CIN under specified conditions, while the policy excerpt does not address those missing data elements. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt focuses on an emergency-department prescription scenario and requires next-business-day follow-up with the assigned PCP, which is not stated in the provider manual excerpt. [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]

Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond the emergency-department/PCP-verification scenario and the general instruction to verify directions and consult the prescriber as necessary. [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist/pharmacy to use professional judgment rather than automatically reject a prescription when verification is needed or information is incomplete: “The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement...” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025] and “the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources preserve a verification/legitimacy check before dispensing: the policy says the pharmacy must be able to “verify the prescriptions” with the assigned PCP when available [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025], and the manual allows dispensing only if the pharmacist is “otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is directly about missing member information and says the pharmacist is “not required to enter” missing address, age, or CIN under specified conditions, while the policy excerpt does not address those missing data elements. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt focuses on an emergency-department prescription scenario and requires next-business-day follow-up with the assigned PCP, which is not stated in the provider manual excerpt. [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]

Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond the emergency-department/PCP-verification scenario and the general instruction to verify directions and consult the prescriber as necessary. [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provider manual is the more recent source overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The provided policy excerpts do not specifically define pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing, so the comparison is limited to the grounded text available.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_39.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."
test_id_39.pm_ground_truth: A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.
test_id_39.policy_ground_truth: The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice.

test_id_40.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_40.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacist’s current guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The provider manual says the pharmacist may not need to enter missing address, age, or CIN if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate; the policy excerpts do not provide a comparable missing-information rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The closest policy excerpts only say pharmacists should verify directions and consult the prescriber as necessary, and that the pharmacy is responsible for professional judgment in certain emergency-department prescriptions, but they do not specifically define what to do when required patient or prescriber information is missing: “Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes.” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]; “The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions.” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]

similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist to use judgment or verify information rather than automatically filling without review: “Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes.” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]; “the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie pharmacist action to verification of prescription legitimacy or prescriber-related review: “consult the prescriber, as necessary” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]; “the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual gives a specific rule for missing member information, stating the pharmacist is “not required to enter any of these items” for missing “address, age, or CIN” under stated conditions, while the policy excerpts do not provide that specific missing-information instruction. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]

The policy excerpts focus on other responsibilities—such as verifying directions, consulting the prescriber, and using professional judgment when an assigned PCP is unavailable—rather than on missing patient or prescriber data. [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist to use judgment or verify information rather than automatically filling without review: “Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes.” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]; “the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie pharmacist action to verification of prescription legitimacy or prescriber-related review: “consult the prescriber, as necessary” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]; “the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual gives a specific rule for missing member information, stating the pharmacist is “not required to enter any of these items” for missing “address, age, or CIN” under stated conditions, while the policy excerpts do not provide that specific missing-information instruction. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]

The policy excerpts focus on other responsibilities—such as verifying directions, consulting the prescriber, and using professional judgment when an assigned PCP is unavailable—rather than on missing patient or prescriber data. [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a direct, grounded definition for missing required patient or prescriber information, so the policy side is incomplete for this specific concept. The provider manual is newer overall for this issue (2025-10-01 vs. 2025-01-01) and should guide current practice unless a historical policy question is intended.
