[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The closest policy excerpts only say pharmacists should verify directions and consult the prescriber as necessary, and that the pharmacy is responsible for professional judgment in certain emergency-department prescriptions, but they do not specifically define what to do when required patient or prescriber information is missing: “Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes.” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]; “The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions.” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, if the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, “the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription” when the information “is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled,” or when “The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist to use judgment or verify information rather than automatically filling without review: “Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes.” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]; “the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources tie pharmacist action to verification of prescription legitimacy or prescriber-related review: “consult the prescriber, as necessary” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]; “the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The provider manual gives a specific rule for missing member information, stating the pharmacist is “not required to enter any of these items” for missing “address, age, or CIN” under stated conditions, while the policy excerpts do not provide that specific missing-information instruction. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]", "The policy excerpts focus on other responsibilities—such as verifying directions, consulting the prescriber, and using professional judgment when an assigned PCP is unavailable—rather than on missing patient or prescriber data. [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]"], "caveats": "The provided policy excerpts do not contain a direct, grounded definition for missing required patient or prescriber information, so the policy side is incomplete for this specific concept. The provider manual is newer overall for this issue (2025-10-01 vs. 2025-01-01) and should guide current practice unless a historical policy question is intended. "}
test_id_40.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."
test_id_40.pm_ground_truth: A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.
test_id_40.policy_ground_truth: The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice.

test_id_41.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_41.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, weight-loss drugs are treated as non-reimbursable/excluded when used for weight loss, and this newer provider manual should guide current use by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-01-01 is consistent with that exclusion, so both sources point to the same answer: yes, weight-loss drugs used for weight loss are excluded/non-reimbursable. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025] [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid does not reimburse drugs/drug uses that include weight-loss treatment: “The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance ...” [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]. Earlier policy guidance is explicit that exclusions include “agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain” for both FFS and MMC. [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual says Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the NYS List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, and lists as non-reimbursable examples: “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]. The companion compound guidance also says compounds may not be made for excluded indications, “including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources treat weight-loss-related drug use as outside Medicaid reimbursement: policy says drugs/drug uses are “not reimbursable” and earlier policy excludes “agents when used for the treatment of ... weight loss,” while the provider manual lists “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” and excludes compounds for “drugs to treat weight loss.” [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025] [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources frame the rule as a coverage/reimbursement limitation rather than a clinical prohibition: policy says “not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid,” and the provider manual says “Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs.” [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is newer and more operational for current billing guidance, dated 2025-10-01, while the newest policy chunk is dated 2025-01-01; the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current use. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025] [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt is broader and more direct about excluded indications in general, explicitly naming “agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain,” while the provider manual excerpt shown here is more limited in the visible text and relies on examples plus compound rules that include “drugs to treat weight loss.” [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources treat weight-loss-related drug use as outside Medicaid reimbursement: policy says drugs/drug uses are “not reimbursable” and earlier policy excludes “agents when used for the treatment of ... weight loss,” while the provider manual lists “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” and excludes compounds for “drugs to treat weight loss.” [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025] [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources frame the rule as a coverage/reimbursement limitation rather than a clinical prohibition: policy says “not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid,” and the provider manual says “Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs.” [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is newer and more operational for current billing guidance, dated 2025-10-01, while the newest policy chunk is dated 2025-01-01; the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current use. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025] [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt is broader and more direct about excluded indications in general, explicitly naming “agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain,” while the provider manual excerpt shown here is more limited in the visible text and relies on examples plus compound rules that include “drugs to treat weight loss.” [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The newest policy chunk available is truncated and does not show the full list of examples after “The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance ...” [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]. The provider manual excerpt also shows truncated text on page 28, so the comparison is grounded only in the visible lines provided. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
*Requires additional MEVS.
Denial code “722”: NDC DESI code is invalid.
NDC Excluded from State Plan Coverage
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_41.ground_truth:
"The manual provides no specific examples of modern weight loss drugs (like GLP-1 agonists). It relies entirely on the general categorical exclusion, which could be open to interpretation with newer medications.

The examples given are: Wegovy, Ozempic, Qsymia, and Mounjaro. This is the most significant clarification, as it removes all ambiguity regarding these popular medications."

• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

prepared and submitted for payment.

for cosmetic purposes.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_42.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for weight loss, so the provider manual should guide current handling of this issue by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-01-01 is consistent and also treats drugs used to treat weight loss as non-reimbursable/excluded. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025] [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for weight loss: “The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance ...” [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]. Earlier policy guidance is explicit that exclusions include “agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain” for both FFS and MMC. [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual evidence says Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list and gives as non-reimbursable examples “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]. The same manual also states compounds may not be made for excluded indications, “including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources treat weight-loss-related drug use as outside Medicaid reimbursement: policy says “agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain” are excluded, and the provider manual says drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable. [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources tie the exclusion to Medicaid reimbursement limits rather than creating a separate benefit category: policy says the items are “not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid,” and the provider manual says Medicaid “only provides reimbursement” for drugs on the reimbursable list. [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The provider manual is more current and more operationally specific: it states the reimbursement-list rule and adds that compounds may not be made for excluded indications, “including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss.” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025]", "The policy excerpts are older and less detailed in the newest chunk; the January 2025 policy excerpt references examples of non-reimbursable drugs/drug uses but the provided text is truncated, so the clearest explicit weight-loss exclusion appears in the April 2022 policy update. [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025] [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022]"], "caveats": "The January 2025 policy chunk is truncated in the provided excerpt, so it does not fully display the weight-loss example text; the April 2022 policy update provides the clearest explicit policy wording. test_id_42.ground_truth:
"The manual provides no specific examples of modern weight loss drugs (like GLP-1 agonists). It relies entirely on the general categorical exclusion, which could be open to interpretation with newer medications.

The examples given are: Wegovy, Ozempic, Qsymia, and Mounjaro. This is the most significant clarification, as it removes all ambiguity regarding these popular medications."
test_id_42.pm_ground_truth: The manual provides no specific examples of modern weight loss drugs (like GLP-1 agonists). It relies entirely on the general categorical exclusion, which could be open to interpretation with newer medications.
test_id_42.policy_ground_truth: The examples given are: Wegovy, Ozempic, Qsymia, and Mounjaro. This is the most significant clarification, as it removes all ambiguity regarding these popular medications.

test_id_43.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_43.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, weight loss drugs are treated as non-reimbursable/excluded when used for weight loss, and this provider manual should receive more weight by default because it is newer than the newest policy source dated 2025-01-01. The policy sources are consistent with that exclusion, so the current guidance is that weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit when used for weight loss. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025]; [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid does not reimburse drugs/drug uses that include weight loss: “The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance ...” [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]. Earlier policy guidance is explicit that exclusions include “agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain” [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022], and compounds may not be made for “excluded indications ... including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss” [mu_no4_apr24_pr.pdf::0017 — Apr 01, 2024].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual states Medicaid reimburses only drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list and gives examples of non-reimbursable drugs/drug uses, including “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also states compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for “NYS Medicaid excluded indications” and that compounds may not be made for “drugs to treat weight loss” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources treat weight loss-related drug use as excluded/non-reimbursable: policy says “agents when used for the treatment of ... weight loss” are excluded, and the provider manual says drugs/drug uses are not reimbursable and compounds may not be made for “drugs to treat weight loss” [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie the exclusion to Medicaid reimbursement limits rather than creating a separate covered benefit category: policy says such drugs/drug uses are “not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid,” and the provider manual says Medicaid “only provides reimbursement” for drugs on the reimbursable list [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is newer and therefore should guide current operations more heavily: provider manual effective date is “2025-10-01,” while the newest policy source is “2025-01-01” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025]; [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts are more explicit about the exclusion wording for weight loss drugs, stating “agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain,” while the provider manual excerpt shown is broader and references non-reimbursable “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” and excluded indications/compounds [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources treat weight loss-related drug use as excluded/non-reimbursable: policy says “agents when used for the treatment of ... weight loss” are excluded, and the provider manual says drugs/drug uses are not reimbursable and compounds may not be made for “drugs to treat weight loss” [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie the exclusion to Medicaid reimbursement limits rather than creating a separate covered benefit category: policy says such drugs/drug uses are “not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid,” and the provider manual says Medicaid “only provides reimbursement” for drugs on the reimbursable list [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is newer and therefore should guide current operations more heavily: provider manual effective date is “2025-10-01,” while the newest policy source is “2025-01-01” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025]; [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts are more explicit about the exclusion wording for weight loss drugs, stating “agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain,” while the provider manual excerpt shown is broader and references non-reimbursable “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” and excluded indications/compounds [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The newest policy chunk provided is only a partial sentence (“The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance ...”), so the policy definition relies on older policy excerpts for the explicit weight loss wording [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]; [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
*Requires additional MEVS.
Denial code “722”: NDC DESI code is invalid.
NDC Excluded from State Plan Coverage
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

test_id_46.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost, so the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 says 340B drugs must be identified with the UD modifier on NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims, but it does not state the reimbursement amount.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says: "Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] It also says: "If the NDC is not referenced on a claim, NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] Earlier policy guidance says 340B drug claims must be "properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the appropriate fields" and that 340B claims are "required to be properly identified as 340B" and "submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider" for FFS claims. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6:4-5 — May 01, 2019] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual source states in the pricing methodology table: "340B** Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18" and indicates that claims pay at the listed reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025] It also requires on Medicaid 340B drug claims that "409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost" and identifies 340B claim fields in the NCPDP format. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual further explains that "Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts" for 340B drugs. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require 340B claims to be identified as 340B: the policy says "must be identified by appending the UD modifier" and the manual requires 340B claim fields/identifiers in the NCPDP format. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to 340B acquisition cost rather than a generic drug price: the policy says claims are "submitted at the 340B acquisition cost" and the manual lists "340B Acquisition Cost" as the reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is explicit about reimbursement amount/methodology for 340B drugs: "340B** Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18," whereas the policy excerpts do not state a reimbursement amount for 340B drugs. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022]

The policy excerpt focuses on claim identification and NDC reporting, stating "If the NDC is not referenced on a claim, NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug," while the provider manual excerpt focuses on pricing fields and duplicate-discount rules. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require 340B claims to be identified as 340B: the policy says "must be identified by appending the UD modifier" and the manual requires 340B claim fields/identifiers in the NCPDP format. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to 340B acquisition cost rather than a generic drug price: the policy says claims are "submitted at the 340B acquisition cost" and the manual lists "340B Acquisition Cost" as the reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is explicit about reimbursement amount/methodology for 340B drugs: "340B** Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18," whereas the policy excerpts do not state a reimbursement amount for 340B drugs. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022]

The policy excerpt focuses on claim identification and NDC reporting, stating "If the NDC is not referenced on a claim, NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug," while the provider manual excerpt focuses on pricing fields and duplicate-discount rules. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The policy excerpts provided do not contain a complete reimbursement rule for 340B drugs, so there is insufficient grounded evidence in the provided policy documents to define the reimbursement amount for policy beyond identification and acquisition-cost submission requirements. The provider manual is newer and should guide current reimbursement handling for this issue.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.

Any pharmacy that submits a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling
price, will be returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code “708: Exceeds NY
Allowed Maximum” and the NCPDP Reject code "23: M/I Ingredient Cost.” The pharmacy would then have
to resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost.

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

test_id_46.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
test_id_46.pm_ground_truth: Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.
test_id_46.policy_ground_truth: drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_47.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost, and this newer provider manual should guide current billing guidance by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 is consistent in requiring 340B drugs to be identified with the UD modifier, but it does not state the reimbursement amount as clearly as the provider manual.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents require 340B drugs to be identified on NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims by appending the UD modifier: "Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] Earlier policy guidance also says 340B drug claims must be properly identified as 340B and submitted at cost in the appropriate fields: "340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the appropriate fields." [New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6:4-5 — May 01, 2019]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual states that 340B drugs are reimbursed at acquisition cost: "340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025] It also requires specific claim fields for 340B drug claims, including "409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost" and notes that claim-level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims are fraudulent billing. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require 340B claims to be identified as 340B: policy says drugs obtained via the 340B program "must be identified by appending the UD modifier" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022], and the provider manual requires 340B claim fields to reflect "340B Acquisition Cost" and related 340B identifiers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32, 38 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie 340B reimbursement/claim submission to cost-based reporting rather than ordinary pricing: policy says 340B claims must be "submitted at costs in the appropriate fields" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6:4-5 — May 01, 2019], and the provider manual says 340B drugs reimburse at "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more explicit about reimbursement amount, stating 340B drugs reimburse at "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt only says 340B drugs must be identified with the UD modifier and does not clearly state the reimbursement rate in the provided 2022 excerpt [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022].

The policy excerpts include an older operational reminder that 340B claims must be "properly identified as 340B" and submitted with costs in the correct fields [New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6:4-5 — May 01, 2019], while the newer provider manual adds specific NCPDP field instructions and a fraud warning for non-340B claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require 340B claims to be identified as 340B: policy says drugs obtained via the 340B program "must be identified by appending the UD modifier" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022], and the provider manual requires 340B claim fields to reflect "340B Acquisition Cost" and related 340B identifiers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32, 38 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie 340B reimbursement/claim submission to cost-based reporting rather than ordinary pricing: policy says 340B claims must be "submitted at costs in the appropriate fields" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6:4-5 — May 01, 2019], and the provider manual says 340B drugs reimburse at "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more explicit about reimbursement amount, stating 340B drugs reimburse at "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt only says 340B drugs must be identified with the UD modifier and does not clearly state the reimbursement rate in the provided 2022 excerpt [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022].

The policy excerpts include an older operational reminder that 340B claims must be "properly identified as 340B" and submitted with costs in the correct fields [New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6:4-5 — May 01, 2019], while the newer provider manual adds specific NCPDP field instructions and a fraud warning for non-340B claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a clear reimbursement-rate statement for 340B drugs, so the reimbursement answer is grounded primarily in the newer provider manual. The policy and provider manual are not fully parallel on this point, so the provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
4

Pharmacy
Reminder: 340B Providers, Covered Entities, and Contract Pharmacies
A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.
The following fields are required on each Medicaid 340B drug claim submitted NCPDP format:

Not applicable

to Cash Customers Customers

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any pharmacy that submits a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling
price, will be returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code “708: Exceeds NY
Allowed Maximum” and the NCPDP Reject code "23: M/I Ingredient Cost.” The pharmacy would then have
to resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

The pricing methodology is systematically determined as follows:
Drug Type If NADAC is available If NADAC is unavailable, Professional Dispensing Fee*
reimburse at: reimburse at:
Lower of NADAC, FUL, Lower of WAC – 17.5%,
Generics SMAC or U&C FUL, SMAC, or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC or
Brands U&C Lower of WAC or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC, FUL,
OTCs SMAC or U&C Lower of WAC, FUL, $10.18
SMAC, or U&C
340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18

Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
