test_id_47.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
test_id_47.pm_ground_truth: Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.
test_id_47.policy_ground_truth: drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_48.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost, so the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 says 340B drugs must be identified with the UD modifier on NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims, but it does not state the reimbursement amount.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says: “Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] Earlier policy guidance also says 340B drug claims must be “properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the appropriate fields” and that claims with incorrect data may be affected, but it does not provide a reimbursement formula in the provided excerpts. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6:4-5 — May 01, 2019]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence states the pricing methodology for “340B**” drugs is “Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost” and, when NADAC is unavailable, reimbursement is also shown as “340B Acquisition Cost $10.18” in the table. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also says the required claim fields for Medicaid 340B drug claims include “409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost” and notes that “applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is considered fraudulent billing.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require 340B claims to be identified as 340B: the policy says to “append[] the UD modifier to the 340B drug line” and the manual requires 340B claim fields such as “420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20” and “423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to the drug’s cost basis rather than a standard retail amount: the policy says 340B claims must be “submitted at costs in the appropriate fields,” and the manual states 340B drugs reimburse at “Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost.” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6:4-5 — May 01, 2019; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual gives an explicit reimbursement methodology for 340B drugs—“Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost”—while the policy excerpts do not state the reimbursement amount. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022]

The policy excerpt focuses on claim identification for NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims—“appending the UD modifier”—whereas the provider manual focuses on pricing and claim field requirements, including “409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require 340B claims to be identified as 340B: the policy says to “append[] the UD modifier to the 340B drug line” and the manual requires 340B claim fields such as “420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20” and “423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to the drug’s cost basis rather than a standard retail amount: the policy says 340B claims must be “submitted at costs in the appropriate fields,” and the manual states 340B drugs reimburse at “Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost.” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6:4-5 — May 01, 2019; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual gives an explicit reimbursement methodology for 340B drugs—“Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost”—while the policy excerpts do not state the reimbursement amount. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022]

The policy excerpt focuses on claim identification for NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims—“appending the UD modifier”—whereas the provider manual focuses on pricing and claim field requirements, including “409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for the policy documents as a reimbursement rule; the policy excerpts only show identification and proper cost submission requirements, not the actual reimbursement amount. The provider manual is newer and should guide current reimbursement handling for this issue unless the user is asking about historical policy.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:

Pharmacy
Reminder: 340B Providers, Covered Entities, and Contract Pharmacies
A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.

Not applicable

*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any pharmacy that submits a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling
price, will be returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code “708: Exceeds NY
Allowed Maximum” and the NCPDP Reject code "23: M/I Ingredient Cost.” The pharmacy would then have
to resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 says 340B drugs must be identified with the UD modifier on NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims, but it does not state the reimbursement amount. test_id_50.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."

It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_51.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, home infusion drugs and supplies are not covered under a bundled payment and must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit; this provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 is older and addresses related drug coverage/billing rules, but it does not specifically define home infusion billing in the provided excerpt.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says NYS Medicaid FFS covers certain drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by the compendia for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service: “NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01] The older policy excerpt also assigns billing responsibility to the pharmacy: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt states: “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] It further says: “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] The manual also notes that NYS Medicaid recognizes home infusion drugs may be available through both benefit types: “NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources tie coverage to Medicaid benefit/payment rules rather than a single bundled home-infusion payment: the provider manual says there is no bundled payment for home infusion treatments [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], and the policy excerpt says drugs are covered under NYS Medicaid FFS when they meet specified indication criteria [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

Both sources place billing on the provider/pharmacy side rather than the member: the policy excerpt says “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], and the provider manual says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment for “drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments,” with billing through pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpt provided is broader and addresses drugs administered in a practitioner’s office, not home infusion specifically [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

The policy excerpt explicitly assigns billing responsibility to “the pharmacy” and says it must bill “the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” as appropriate [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], while the provider manual excerpt does not assign billing responsibility to a single entity in the quoted text; it only states the claim must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources tie coverage to Medicaid benefit/payment rules rather than a single bundled home-infusion payment: the provider manual says there is no bundled payment for home infusion treatments [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], and the policy excerpt says drugs are covered under NYS Medicaid FFS when they meet specified indication criteria [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

Both sources place billing on the provider/pharmacy side rather than the member: the policy excerpt says “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], and the provider manual says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment for “drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments,” with billing through pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpt provided is broader and addresses drugs administered in a practitioner’s office, not home infusion specifically [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

The policy excerpt explicitly assigns billing responsibility to “the pharmacy” and says it must bill “the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” as appropriate [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], while the provider manual excerpt does not assign billing responsibility to a single entity in the quoted text; it only states the claim must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents specifically as home infusion coverage; the policy excerpts are related but not home-infusion-specific. The provider manual is the newer and more directly applicable source for the home infusion question.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_52.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_52.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, home infusion drugs and supplies are not covered under a bundled payment and must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit; this provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 addresses billing responsibility generally, stating the pharmacy must bill timely to the appropriate party, but it does not specifically define home infusion coverage.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence in the provided chunks does not specifically define home infusion drug coverage; it only states generally that “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]. For billing responsibility, policy says “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual states that “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments” and that “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. It also says NYS Medicaid recognizes “certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources place billing responsibility on the pharmacy side for at least some drug claims: policy says “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], and the provider manual says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources recognize coverage for drugs administered by a provider or health care professional in a Medicaid context: policy covers drugs administered by “subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office” under specified indication rules [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], and the provider manual refers to “home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual gives direct home-infusion-specific coverage guidance, stating there is no bundled payment and that home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], while the policy chunks do not specifically define home infusion coverage and instead discuss broader injectable-drug coverage in a practitioner’s office [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

The policy source explicitly assigns billing responsibility to “the pharmacy” and says it must bill “to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], whereas the provider manual frames billing as a benefit-selection rule for home infusion drugs and supplies (“pharmacy or medical benefit”) without naming the pharmacy as the responsible biller in the quoted text [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources place billing responsibility on the pharmacy side for at least some drug claims: policy says “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], and the provider manual says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources recognize coverage for drugs administered by a provider or health care professional in a Medicaid context: policy covers drugs administered by “subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office” under specified indication rules [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], and the provider manual refers to “home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual gives direct home-infusion-specific coverage guidance, stating there is no bundled payment and that home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], while the policy chunks do not specifically define home infusion coverage and instead discuss broader injectable-drug coverage in a practitioner’s office [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

The policy source explicitly assigns billing responsibility to “the pharmacy” and says it must bill “to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], whereas the provider manual frames billing as a benefit-selection rule for home infusion drugs and supplies (“pharmacy or medical benefit”) without naming the pharmacy as the responsible biller in the quoted text [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: The policy excerpts provided do not contain a home-infusion-specific definition, so the policy-side home infusion coverage question is only partially grounded; the clearest current guidance comes from the 2025-10-01 provider manual. The policy billing statement is older (2020-07-01) than the newest policy coverage excerpt (2022-07-01), so it should be treated as background unless you are specifically asking about historical billing guidance.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

The drug must be dispensed by a licensed pharmacy. It implies that the pharmacy is responsible for billing for the drug product itself.
The updates implicitly solidify the rule that a pharmacy cannot bill for the professional nursing component.

test_id_53.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_53.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, home infusion drugs and supplies are not covered under a bundled payment and must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit; this provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 is older and addresses related drug coverage and billing responsibilities, but it does not specifically define home infusion billing in the same detail.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid FFS covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by the compendia, and if no recognized indication exists, an approved IRB protocol may be used [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022]. Older policy guidance also states that “it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond these related billing and drug-coverage statements.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual states: “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also says NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for “certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources tie drug coverage to Medicaid billing pathways rather than a single bundled home-infusion payment: the provider manual says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], and policy says the pharmacy must bill “to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020].

Both sources recognize coverage for drugs administered by a health care provider/practitioner under Medicaid rules: policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office for FDA-approved or supported medically accepted indications [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022], and the provider manual says home infusion drugs requiring administration by a health care provider may be available through both the medical and pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment for “drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not specifically address home infusion bundled payment and instead discuss broader administered-drug coverage criteria [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022].

The policy excerpt assigns billing responsibility to the pharmacy, stating “it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], while the provider manual frames billing as a benefit-selection rule (“must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit”) and does not assign billing responsibility to a specific party in the provided excerpt [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources tie drug coverage to Medicaid billing pathways rather than a single bundled home-infusion payment: the provider manual says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], and policy says the pharmacy must bill “to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020].

Both sources recognize coverage for drugs administered by a health care provider/practitioner under Medicaid rules: policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office for FDA-approved or supported medically accepted indications [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022], and the provider manual says home infusion drugs requiring administration by a health care provider may be available through both the medical and pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment for “drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not specifically address home infusion bundled payment and instead discuss broader administered-drug coverage criteria [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022].

The policy excerpt assigns billing responsibility to the pharmacy, stating “it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], while the provider manual frames billing as a benefit-selection rule (“must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit”) and does not assign billing responsibility to a specific party in the provided excerpt [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The policy excerpts are older than the provider manual and do not contain a direct home-infusion-specific billing rule; the provider manual is the more current source for this issue and should guide current billing decisions unless the question is historical [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022].

test_id_54.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_54.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, home infusion drugs and supplies are not covered under a bundled payment and must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit; this newer provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 addresses drugs administered in a practitioner’s office, not home infusion specifically, and is therefore only partial background for this question.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid FFS covers “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office” for “FDA approved indications” or medically accepted indications supported by the compendia in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022] Older policy language similarly states that “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — Feb 01, 2019] The policy chunks provided do not specifically define home infusion drugs or who bills for them. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual states: “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] It further says: “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also says NYS Medicaid recognizes home infusion drugs as needing administration by a health care provider and available “by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit,” subject to pharmacy-benefit eligibility criteria. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources describe coverage for drugs administered by injection/infusion routes rather than oral-only dispensing: policy covers drugs administered “by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022], and the manual addresses “home infusion drugs” requiring administration by a health care provider [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie coverage to Medicaid benefit/payment rules rather than a single universal payment method: policy discusses Medicaid FFS coverage for qualifying administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022], while the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as “a pharmacy or medical benefit” and not under a bundled payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is explicit that there is no bundled payment for home infusion and that home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not mention bundled payment or home infusion billing responsibility. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.

The policy excerpts focus on coverage criteria for drugs administered in a practitioner’s office and on FDA-approved or compendia-supported indications [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022], whereas the provider manual specifically addresses home infusion treatments and billing as pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources describe coverage for drugs administered by injection/infusion routes rather than oral-only dispensing: policy covers drugs administered “by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022], and the manual addresses “home infusion drugs” requiring administration by a health care provider [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie coverage to Medicaid benefit/payment rules rather than a single universal payment method: policy discusses Medicaid FFS coverage for qualifying administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022], while the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as “a pharmacy or medical benefit” and not under a bundled payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is explicit that there is no bundled payment for home infusion and that home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not mention bundled payment or home infusion billing responsibility. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.

The policy excerpts focus on coverage criteria for drugs administered in a practitioner’s office and on FDA-approved or compendia-supported indications [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022], whereas the provider manual specifically addresses home infusion treatments and billing as pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not directly address home infusion drugs or identify the billing party for home infusion; the provider manual is the controlling, more recent source for current home infusion billing guidance. The policy material is useful only as background on coverage of administered drugs generally.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
