provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_55.ground_truth:
The drug must be dispensed by a licensed pharmacy. It implies that the pharmacy is responsible for billing for the drug product itself.
The updates implicitly solidify the rule that a pharmacy cannot bill for the professional nursing component.

test_id_56.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, naloxone reimbursement is addressed through pharmacy billing methodology: the pharmacy is reimbursed for the medication when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology, and drug and administration claims must be submitted separately. The newest policy source in the provided policy documents is older and does not contain grounded evidence about naloxone reimbursement, so the provider manual should guide current handling of this issue by default [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The newest policy-related chunks provided do not mention naloxone reimbursement; the closest reimbursement language is unrelated and states, "Reimbursement for the cost of the vaccine for members 19 years of age and older will be made at no more than the actual acquisition cost to the pharmacy." [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:10 — Aug 01, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that, for pharmacy billing, "If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately." It also states, "For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources use reimbursement language tied to Medicaid payment rules rather than defining naloxone itself; the policy excerpt says, "Reimbursement for the cost of the vaccine..." [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:10 — Aug 01, 2021], and the provider manual says, "The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources are limited to reimbursement mechanics in the provided excerpts and do not supply a naloxone-specific reimbursement rate or amount [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:10 — Aug 01, 2021; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is newer and directly addresses pharmacy reimbursement mechanics for medication billing, stating, "The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy documents provided do not contain grounded evidence defining naloxone reimbursement; the only reimbursement language found is unrelated vaccine reimbursement, "Reimbursement for the cost of the vaccine for members 19 years of age and older will be made at no more than the actual acquisition cost to the pharmacy." [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:10 — Aug 01, 2021].
comparison: Both sources use reimbursement language tied to Medicaid payment rules rather than defining naloxone itself; the policy excerpt says, "Reimbursement for the cost of the vaccine..." [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:10 — Aug 01, 2021], and the provider manual says, "The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources are limited to reimbursement mechanics in the provided excerpts and do not supply a naloxone-specific reimbursement rate or amount [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:10 — Aug 01, 2021; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is newer and directly addresses pharmacy reimbursement mechanics for medication billing, stating, "The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy documents provided do not contain grounded evidence defining naloxone reimbursement; the only reimbursement language found is unrelated vaccine reimbursement, "Reimbursement for the cost of the vaccine for members 19 years of age and older will be made at no more than the actual acquisition cost to the pharmacy." [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:10 — Aug 01, 2021].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not include naloxone-specific reimbursement guidance, so the policy side is insufficient for a direct definition. The provider manual is the more recent and more relevant source for current reimbursement handling in the provided materials [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.

test_id_56.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."
test_id_56.pm_ground_truth: The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.
test_id_56.policy_ground_truth: The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

[Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023]

The provider manual gives direct billing/reimbursement instructions for the medication, stating, “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpts provided do not mention naloxone, NDC billing, or medication reimbursement. [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023]

The provider manual is newer and should guide current interpretation for this issue, but the excerpt still does not explicitly name naloxone; it speaks generally about medication reimbursement and billing methodology.

test_id_57.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."

test_id_57.policy_ground_truth: The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

test_id_58.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, naloxone is reimbursed when the drug claim is submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI and billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology; this provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source in the provided excerpts is dated 2023-03-01, but it does not define naloxone reimbursement, so it is only background here.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The newest policy excerpt provided is about HCBS authorization, stating: “MMCPs will reimburse for initial authorization of 96 units or a total of 24 hours of service for a period of 60 days from the time that the MMCP receives notification from the HCBS provider.” [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says that for a drug claim, “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” It also says, “The claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources use reimbursement language tied to Medicaid payment processes, but only the provider manual applies that language to a drug claim; the policy excerpt does not address naloxone reimbursement. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023]

Both sources are Medicaid-related, but the provided policy excerpt concerns HCBS authorization rather than medication reimbursement. [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual gives direct billing/reimbursement instructions for a medication claim: “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpt does not provide any naloxone-specific reimbursement rule. [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023]

The policy excerpt’s reimbursement rule is for HCBS service authorization—“MMCPs will reimburse for initial authorization of 96 units or a total of 24 hours of service for a period of 60 days”—which is not a naloxone drug reimbursement rule. [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023] The provider manual excerpt instead addresses pharmacy drug billing and prescriber ID requirements. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources use reimbursement language tied to Medicaid payment processes, but only the provider manual applies that language to a drug claim; the policy excerpt does not address naloxone reimbursement. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023]

Both sources are Medicaid-related, but the provided policy excerpt concerns HCBS authorization rather than medication reimbursement. [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual gives direct billing/reimbursement instructions for a medication claim: “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpt does not provide any naloxone-specific reimbursement rule. [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023]

The policy excerpt’s reimbursement rule is for HCBS service authorization—“MMCPs will reimburse for initial authorization of 96 units or a total of 24 hours of service for a period of 60 days”—which is not a naloxone drug reimbursement rule. [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023] The provider manual excerpt instead addresses pharmacy drug billing and prescriber ID requirements. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain grounded evidence defining naloxone reimbursement, so the policy side cannot be used to answer the question directly. The provider manual excerpt is the more recent and controlling source for the reimbursement mechanics shown here, but the excerpt does not mention naloxone by name; it only states general medication billing/reimbursement rules. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.

test_id_59.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."

They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

test_id_60.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, naloxone reimbursement is addressed as part of pharmacy drug billing methodology, and this source should receive more weight by default because it is newer than the newest policy source dated 2023-03-01. The policy excerpts provided do not define naloxone reimbursement specifically, so current guidance is driven by the provider manual.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The newest policy source provided is a 2023-03-01 HCBS manual excerpt stating only that “MMCPs will reimburse for initial authorization of 96 units or a total of 24 hours of service for a period of 60 days from the time that the MMCP receives notification from the HCBS provider.” [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01 states that “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” It also states that “If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately” and that “For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources use reimbursement language tied to Medicaid payment and provider billing, but only the provider manual gives drug-specific billing instructions for reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023].

Both sources describe reimbursement in operational terms rather than as a standalone definition, but the policy excerpt is about HCBS service authorization while the provider manual is about pharmacy drug claims [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is newer and specifically addresses pharmacy reimbursement for a medication billed via NDC, while the policy excerpts do not mention naloxone or pharmacy drug reimbursement at all [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023].

The policy excerpt only addresses MMCP reimbursement for “initial authorization of 96 units or a total of 24 hours of service for a period of 60 days,” which is unrelated to naloxone reimbursement [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023].
comparison: Both sources use reimbursement language tied to Medicaid payment and provider billing, but only the provider manual gives drug-specific billing instructions for reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023].

Both sources describe reimbursement in operational terms rather than as a standalone definition, but the policy excerpt is about HCBS service authorization while the provider manual is about pharmacy drug claims [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is newer and specifically addresses pharmacy reimbursement for a medication billed via NDC, while the policy excerpts do not mention naloxone or pharmacy drug reimbursement at all [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023].

The policy excerpt only addresses MMCP reimbursement for “initial authorization of 96 units or a total of 24 hours of service for a period of 60 days,” which is unrelated to naloxone reimbursement [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023].
answers.caveats: The provided policy chunks do not contain grounded evidence about naloxone reimbursement specifically, so the policy side cannot be used to define this concept. The provider manual excerpt is the controlling current guidance among the provided sources because it is newer (2025-10-01), but the excerpt is partial and does not show the full reimbursement methodology text.

made at no more than the actual acquisition cost to the pharmacy.

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
